title: How Many Glasses of Water Per Day? The Hydration and Eye Health Connection
description: How much water should you drink daily — and what does hydration have to do with your eyes? Explore the link between water intake, dry eyes, and vision health.
tldr: Most adults need 8-10 glasses (2-2.5 litres) of water daily. Dehydration directly affects eye health by reducing tear production, worsening dry eye symptoms, and contributing to eye strain. Edmonton's dry climate makes hydration especially important for comfortable vision.

definition.text: Adequate water intake supports healthy tear film production, which is essential for clear, comfortable vision. Chronic dehydration is a contributing factor to dry eye syndrome, blurred vision, and eye fatigue.

sections.h2: How Much Water You Actually Need
sections.content:
The "8 glasses a day" guideline is a reasonable starting point, but your actual needs depend on several factors:

  • Body size: A general formula is 30-35 mL per kilogram of body weight. A 70 kg person needs roughly 2.1-2.5 litres daily.
  • Activity level: Physical activity increases water loss through sweat. Add 1-2 extra glasses for every hour of moderate exercise.
  • Climate: Edmonton's prairie climate is particularly dry, especially during winter when indoor heating pulls moisture from the air. Albertans often need more water than the baseline recommendation.
  • Diet: About 20% of your daily water comes from food (fruits, vegetables, soups). Coffee and tea count toward hydration despite mild diuretic effects.

Health Canada and the Dietitians of Canada recommend roughly 2.2 litres (about 9 cups) for women and 3 litres (about 12 cups) for men as adequate daily intake, including water from all sources.

sections.h2: Why Your Eyes Care About Water Intake
sections.content:
Your eyes are surprisingly dependent on hydration. Here's the direct connection:

Tear Film Production

The tear film that coats your cornea is mostly water. This thin layer keeps your eyes lubricated, washes away debris, and maintains the smooth optical surface you need for clear vision. When you're dehydrated, tear production decreases and the tears you do produce are more concentrated (saltier), which irritates the eye surface.

Vitreous Humour

The jelly-like substance filling the inside of your eye (vitreous humour) is 99% water. While mild dehydration won't shrink your eyes, chronic under-hydration can affect the eye's internal pressure and overall comfort.

Blood Flow to the Eyes

Your eyes receive nutrients and oxygen through blood vessels. Dehydration thickens the blood slightly, reducing efficient circulation to the delicate capillaries that serve your retina and optic nerve.

Dry eye is one of the most common complaints eye care professionals hear — and dehydration is one of the most overlooked contributing factors.

Symptoms of dehydration-related dry eye:

  • Gritty, sandy feeling in the eyes
  • Burning or stinging sensations
  • Blurred vision that improves after blinking
  • Eye fatigue, especially during screen use
  • Excessive tearing (paradoxical — your eyes overcompensate for dryness with low-quality reflex tears)

In Edmonton, dry eye is especially common during winter months when humidity drops below 20% indoors. The combination of cold outdoor air, heated indoor air, and screen time creates a triple threat for your tear film.

If increasing your water intake doesn't resolve persistent dry eye symptoms, it's worth getting a comprehensive eye exam at Charm Optical. Your optometrist can evaluate your tear film quality and recommend targeted treatments.

sections.h2: Hydration Tips for Better Eye Comfort
sections.content:
Practical ways to stay hydrated and keep your eyes comfortable:

  • Front-load your water intake. Drink 2-3 glasses before noon. Waiting until you feel thirsty means you're already mildly dehydrated.
  • Keep water visible. A water bottle on your desk serves as a constant reminder. People who keep water within reach drink 25-50% more throughout the day.
  • Use a humidifier in winter. Edmonton's winter air drops indoor humidity to 15-25%. A humidifier in your bedroom and workspace keeps ambient moisture up, reducing tear evaporation.
  • Eat water-rich foods. Cucumbers, watermelon, oranges, celery, and strawberries are 85-96% water and contribute meaningfully to your daily intake.
  • Limit alcohol and excessive caffeine. Both are diuretics that increase water loss. One extra glass of water for each alcoholic drink helps offset the effect.

When comparing options, consider your daily routine and visual priorities rather than just price. The "best" choice is the one that matches how you actually use your eyes throughout the day. Someone who works on a computer for 8 hours has very different needs than someone who primarily drives or reads. An honest conversation with your optometrist about your daily visual demands will point you toward the right option far more effectively than online reviews.

Keep in mind that you're not locked into one choice forever. Many people own multiple pairs for different situations — dedicated computer glasses for the office, progressives for daily wear, and prescription sunglasses for driving. Charm Optical carries 500+ frames across every style and price point, making it easy to build a collection that covers all your visual needs. Their opticians can advise which lens types pair best with each frame style. Same-day glasses are available for many single vision prescriptions.

sections.h2: Screen Time, Dehydration, and Your Eyes
sections.content:
People who work on screens all day face a double problem: they blink less (reducing tear film replenishment) and often drink less water (forgetting to hydrate while focused on work).

A few combined strategies:

  • The 20-20-20 rule plus a sip: Every 20 minutes, look at something 20 feet away for 20 seconds — and take a drink of water while you're at it.
  • Blue light glasses can help too: While hydration addresses the tear film component, blue light filtering lenses can reduce the high-energy light component of screen fatigue.
  • Position your screen below eye level. Looking slightly downward narrows the eye opening and reduces the exposed surface area, slowing tear evaporation.

The combination of proper hydration, regular blinking breaks, and appropriate eyewear addresses screen-related eye discomfort from multiple angles simultaneously.

Your eyes are one of the most important tools you have, and they deserve proper care and correction. The technology behind modern eyewear has advanced remarkably in recent years — from digital freeform progressive lenses that customize the optics to your exact wearing position, to lightweight titanium frames that weigh less than a coin, to anti-reflective coatings that virtually eliminate glare. Understanding these options empowers you to make better choices about your eye health and get glasses that genuinely improve your quality of life rather than just meeting a basic need.

Regular eye exams are the foundation of good vision care. The Canadian Association of Optometrists recommends exams every one to two years for adults and annually for children, seniors over 65, and anyone with diabetes or a family history of eye disease. A comprehensive exam does far more than measure your prescription — your optometrist checks the health of your retina, optic nerve, and blood vessels, catching conditions like glaucoma and macular degeneration in their earliest stages. Many serious eye conditions have no noticeable symptoms until significant damage has occurred, making preventive exams essential.

The best next step is always a professional eye exam. Your optometrist can evaluate your specific needs and recommend the right combination of lenses, coatings, and frames for your lifestyle and budget. In Alberta, most employer insurance plans cover comprehensive eye exams, and children under 18 and adults over 65 receive coverage through Alberta Health.

Charm Optical at 5035 Ellerslie Rd SW in South Edmonton offers thorough eye exams with experienced optometrists, a selection of over 500 designer frames from brands like Ray-Ban, Oakley, and Versace, and same-day glasses for many prescription types. They offer direct insurance billing for most Alberta providers, so you only pay the difference at the counter.

faqs.q: Can drinking more water help dry eyes?
faqs.a: Yes — dehydration directly reduces tear production and quality. Increasing water intake to 8-10 glasses daily can improve mild dry eye symptoms. If symptoms persist despite good hydration, visit Charm Optical for a dry eye assessment with an optometrist.

faqs.q: Why are my eyes so dry in Edmonton winters?
faqs.a: Edmonton's winter combines cold, dry outdoor air with heated, low-humidity indoor air. This accelerates tear evaporation. A humidifier, increased water intake, and lubricating eye drops can all help. Your optometrist at Charm Optical can recommend specific solutions for your situation.

faqs.q: Does coffee dehydrate you and cause dry eyes?
faqs.a: Moderate coffee intake (2-4 cups daily) doesn't significantly dehydrate most people — the water content largely offsets the mild diuretic effect. Excessive caffeine consumption beyond 4-5 cups may contribute to dehydration. Balance coffee with water throughout the day.

faqs.q: What lens material is best for strong prescriptions?
faqs.a: High-index lenses (1.67 or 1.74) are best for strong prescriptions because they bend light more efficiently, resulting in thinner and lighter lenses. Standard plastic (CR-39) works well for mild prescriptions. Your optician at Charm Optical can recommend the ideal index based on your prescription strength.

faqs.q: How often should I replace my lenses?
faqs.a: Most optometrists recommend updating your prescription every 1-2 years. Lens coatings (anti-reflective, scratch-resistant) degrade over time even if your prescription hasn't changed. If you notice scratches, haze, or peeling, it's time for new lenses. Book an eye exam at Charm Optical to check your current prescription.
